content.body: A wave is a transfer of energy through a medium (or vacuum) without the permanent transfer of matter. Waves are everywhere: sound, light, water ripples, seismic waves, and radio signals are all waves.

Key wave properties:
Amplitude (A): maximum displacement from rest position. Unit: metres (m)
Wavelength (λ): distance between two consecutive identical points. Unit: metres (m)
Frequency (f): number of complete waves per second. Unit: Hertz (Hz)
Time Period (T): time for one complete wave. T = 1/f. Unit: seconds (s)
Wave Speed (v): v = fλ. Unit: m/s

content.title: Wave Calculations
content.body:
A wave has frequency 50 Hz and wavelength 3 m. Find its speed.
v = fλ = 50 × 3 = 150 m/s

A wave has speed 340 m/s and frequency 170 Hz. Find wavelength.
λ = v/f = 340/170 = 2 m

Types of waves:
Transverse waves: particles vibrate perpendicular to direction of travel. Examples: light, water waves.
Longitudinal waves: particles vibrate parallel to direction of travel. Example: sound.

In longitudinal waves you see compressions (high pressure) and rarefactions (low pressure).

content.title: Sound Waves
content.body:
Sound is a longitudinal wave that travels through a medium.
Speed of sound in air ≈ 340 m/s
Speed of sound in water ≈ 1500 m/s
Speed of sound in steel ≈ 5000 m/s
Sound travels FASTER in denser/more rigid media.
Sound cannot travel in a vacuum (space).

questions.question: What is the unit of frequency?
questions.explanation: Frequency is measured in Hertz (Hz) = waves per second.

questions.question: Wave speed v = ?
questions.explanation: Wave speed = frequency × wavelength (v = fλ).

questions.question: A wave has f=200 Hz and λ=1.7 m. What is its speed?
In which type of wave do particles vibrate parallel to direction of travel?
questions.explanation: Longitudinal waves (like sound) have particles vibrating parallel to wave direction.

questions.question: If T = 0.02 s, what is the frequency?
Can sound travel through a vacuum?
questions.explanation: Sound is a mechanical wave — it needs particles to vibrate. A vacuum has no particles.

questions.question: Light waves are:
questions.explanation: Light (electromagnetic waves) are transverse — they can travel through a vacuum.

questions.question: Amplitude of a wave represents:
questions.explanation: Amplitude is the maximum displacement of particles from their rest position.

questions.question: A wave travels at 300 m/s with wavelength 0.5 m. Find its frequency.
In which medium does sound travel fastest?
questions.explanation: Sound travels faster in denser/more rigid materials. Steel is most rigid of these options.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Electricity is the flow of electric charge. In metal conductors, electrons flow from negative to positive (conventional current flows positive to negative). Understanding current, voltage, and resistance is fundamental to all electronics.

Key quantities:
Electric Current (I): rate of flow of charge. Unit: Ampere (A)
Voltage / Potential Difference (V): energy per unit charge. Unit: Volt (V)
Resistance (R): opposition to current flow. Unit: Ohm (Ω)

Ohm's Law: V = IR
Also: I = V/R  and  R = V/I

content.title: Using Ohm's Law
content.body:
A resistor has V = 12 V and R = 4 Ω. Find the current.
I = V/R = 12/4 = 3 A

A current of 2 A flows through a 6 Ω resistor. Find the voltage.
V = IR = 2 × 6 = 12 V

Series and Parallel Circuits:

SERIES:
• Total resistance: Rₜ = R₁ + R₂ + R₃
• Same current everywhere: I₁ = I₂ = I
• Voltages add: Vₜ = V₁ + V₂

PARALLEL:
• 1/Rₜ = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂
• Same voltage everywhere: V₁ = V₂
• Currents add: Iₜ = I₁ + I₂

Electrical Power and Energy:
Power: P = IV = I²R = V²/R. Unit: Watt (W)
Energy: E = Pt = IVt. Unit: Joule (J)
Electricity bill units: kilowatt-hour (kWh) = 1000 W × 3600 s = 3.6 × 10⁶ J

content.title: Power Calculation
content.body:
A bulb has V = 240 V and I = 0.5 A. Find its power rating.
P = IV = 0.5 × 240 = 120 W

How much energy does it use in 2 hours?
E = Pt = 120 × (2 × 3600) = 864,000 J = 864 kJ

questions.question: State Ohm's Law.
questions.explanation: Ohm's Law: Voltage = Current × Resistance (V = IR).

questions.question: V=24V, R=8Ω. Find I.
The unit of resistance is:
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω).

questions.question: Two resistors 4Ω and 6Ω in series. Total resistance?
questions.explanation: Series: Rₜ = R₁ + R₂ = 4 + 6 = 10 Ω.

questions.question: Two resistors 4Ω and 4Ω in parallel. Total resistance?
Power formula P = ?
questions.explanation: Electrical power P = IV (current × voltage).

questions.question: I=3A, V=9V. Find power.
In a parallel circuit, voltage across each branch is:
questions.explanation: In a parallel circuit, all branches have the same voltage (potential difference).

questions.question: A 1000W heater runs for 3 hours. How many kWh?
questions.explanation: Energy = Power × Time = 1 kW × 3 h = 3 kWh.

questions.question: Increasing resistance in a circuit (with fixed voltage) will:
questions.explanation: I = V/R. If R increases and V is fixed, I decreases.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Ohm's Law: V = IR
V = voltage (volts, V)
I = current (amperes, A)
R = resistance (ohms, Ω)

Resistance in series: R_total = R₁ + R₂ + R₃
Resistance in parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃

Power in circuits:
P = IV = I²R = V²/R
Energy = Pt = IVt

Ammeters measure current (connected in series)
Voltmeters measure voltage (connected in parallel)

content.title: Series vs Parallel
content.body:
Series circuit:
• Same current flows everywhere
• Voltages add up: V_total = V₁+V₂+V₃
• If one component fails, all fail

Parallel circuit:
• Same voltage across each branch
• Currents add up: I_total = I₁+I₂+I₃
• If one branch fails, others work

questions.question: Ohm's Law states V = ...
questions.explanation: Ohm's Law: Voltage = Current × Resistance (V = IR)

questions.question: A 12V battery drives 3A current. Resistance =
Two 6Ω resistors in series. Total resistance =
questions.explanation: Series: R_total = 6+6 = 12 Ω

questions.question: Two 6Ω resistors in parallel. Total resistance =
In a series circuit, if one bulb blows...
questions.explanation: Series circuit has one path. Break anywhere stops all current flow.

questions.question: An ammeter is connected...
questions.explanation: Ammeters must be in series to measure current flowing through a component.

questions.question: Power dissipated by 4Ω resistor carrying 3A =
Voltage across a 10Ω resistor with 2A current =
Household appliances are wired in parallel because...
options: Each works at full voltage independently
questions.answer: Each works at full voltage independently
questions.explanation: Parallel wiring means each appliance gets the full mains voltage and operates independently of others.

questions.question: Which meter is connected in parallel with a component?
questions.explanation: Voltmeters measure voltage across a component and must be connected in parallel (high resistance, takes negligible current).

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: Sound is a longitudinal mechanical wave — particles vibrate in the same direction as the wave travels. Sound requires a medium and cannot travel in vacuum.

Wave properties:
Speed of sound in air ≈ 340 m/s (at 20°C)
Speed of sound in water ≈ 1500 m/s
Speed of sound in steel ≈ 5000 m/s
(Sound travels fastest in solids)

v = fλ, f = 1/T

content.title: Properties of Sound
content.body:
Pitch: determined by frequency. High frequency = high pitch.
Loudness: determined by amplitude. Large amplitude = loud sound.
Quality (timbre): determined by waveform shape.

Hearing range:
Humans: 20 Hz – 20,000 Hz
Ultrasound: above 20,000 Hz (used in medical scans, sonar)
Infrasound: below 20 Hz (elephants, earthquakes)

content.title: Echoes and Sonar
content.body:
Echo: reflection of sound off a surface
Time for echo = 2d/v (where d = distance to reflector)
Sonar (Sound Navigation And Ranging): ship sends ultrasound pulse, measures time for echo to calculate depth of ocean or position of fish/submarines

questions.question: Sound is a...
questions.explanation: Sound waves are longitudinal — particles vibrate parallel to the direction of wave travel.

questions.question: Sound travels fastest in...
questions.explanation: Sound speed increases with density and elasticity: solids>liquids>gases. Fastest in steel (~5000 m/s).

questions.question: Sound cannot travel through vacuum because...
options: It needs particles to vibrate (requires a medium)
questions.answer: It needs particles to vibrate (requires a medium)
questions.explanation: Sound is a mechanical wave — it needs matter (particles) to transmit the vibration. No matter = no sound.

questions.question: The pitch of sound depends on...
questions.explanation: Higher frequency = higher pitch. A violin string vibrates faster (higher frequency) = higher pitched note.

questions.question: Ultrasound frequency is...
questions.explanation: Ultrasound is sound with frequency above the human hearing limit of 20,000 Hz (20 kHz).

questions.question: A ship sends a sonar pulse and hears echo after 4 s. Speed of sound in water = 1500 m/s. Depth of ocean =
questions.explanation: Distance = v×t/2 = 1500×4/2 = 3000 m (divided by 2 because sound travels to bottom and back)

questions.question: Loudness of sound is determined by...
questions.explanation: Larger amplitude = more energy in wave = louder sound.

questions.question: Which medical application uses ultrasound?
questions.explanation: Ultrasound scans use high-frequency sound to image soft tissues safely without ionising radiation.

questions.question: Speed of sound in air ≈
questions.explanation: Sound travels at approximately 340 m/s in air at room temperature.

questions.question: An echo is...
questions.explanation: An echo is the reflected return of a sound wave from a hard surface back to the source.

questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

Every action has an equal reaction

questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Waves & Sound. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Waves & Sound concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Waves & Sound builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Waves & Sound. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Waves & Sound. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Waves & Sound include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Waves & Sound. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
